executive_summary: March 2026 paints a picture of a market where institutional and individual investors are living in different worlds. Institutional investors are split down the middle on most questions — 50-50 on whether stocks will rise, on crash risk, and on valuations — but they're remarkably unified in believing that any dip will bounce back fast. Individual investors, meanwhile, have turned notably gloomier: only a third expect prices to be higher a year from now, and their willingness to buy the dip has dropped sharply. The one thing both groups seem to quietly agree on? The market isn't cheap.

key_findings.headline: Optimism Fades for Individuals
key_findings.interpretation: Institutional investors held steady at a coin-flip 50%, but individual confidence dropped sharply from about 58% to 33%. This may suggest everyday wealthy investors are feeling something — whether it's economic anxiety, political uncertainty, or simply fatigue after a volatile stretch — that hasn't fully reached the professional class.

key_findings.headline: Crash Fears Linger Below Surface
key_findings.interpretation: Half of institutional investors feel confident a crash won't happen, a slight dip from February. Among individuals, only 20% feel safe — meaning four out of five think a crash is at least plausible. That's a notable gap between the pros and the public.

key_findings.headline: Pros Say Buy, Public Hesitates
key_findings.interpretation: Every single institutional respondent believes a 3% drop would fully recover within two days — a remarkable level of conviction. Individual investors moved in the opposite direction, falling from 81% to 40%. One reading of this: professionals may see strong structural support under the market, while individuals may have been burned by recent volatility.

key_findings.headline: Overvaluation Worries Persist Broadly
key_findings.interpretation: Half of institutional investors and only 40% of individuals believe the market is fairly priced or undervalued. It's worth noting that some institutional investors subscribe to market-efficiency theory, where the current price is the correct price by definition — making this question less meaningful for that subset. Still, neither group is exactly pounding the table on bargains.

detailed_analysis:
The standout number this month is that 100% Buy-on-Dips reading among institutional investors. It's like every professional in the room agreeing on a restaurant — unusual and worth paying attention to. One explanation: institutions may see deep liquidity and strong market-making infrastructure that would cushion any sudden sell-off. Another possibility: after recent dips that did recover quickly, they're simply pattern-matching from recent experience. Either way, it's a striking show of unanimity.

Individual investors tell a very different story. Their 1-Year Confidence dropped from about 58% to just 33%, their Buy-on-Dips reading was cut in half, and their Crash Confidence sits at a low 20%. That last number means roughly four in five wealthy individual investors think a meaningful crash is plausible in the next six months. Whether that reflects genuine economic signals they're picking up — or a more emotional response to headlines and portfolio swings — is hard to say from the data alone.

Valuation Confidence ticked up modestly for both groups compared to February, but remains subdued. Only half of institutional investors think the market isn't overvalued, and among individuals it's just 40%. For those who believe markets are generally efficient, this question is almost beside the point — the price is the price. But for those who think markets can drift from fundamentals, these readings could reflect a sense that prices have gotten ahead of earnings or economic reality.

The widening gap between institutional and individual sentiment is perhaps the most important theme. In January, both groups were relatively upbeat. By March, they've diverged sharply on nearly every measure. This could reflect different information sources, different time horizons, or different emotional responses to the same events. History shows that neither group has a monopoly on being right.
market_context: March's surveys were collected as the U.S.-Iran conflict was entering its early weeks — oil was surging, inflation expectations were climbing, and hopes for rate cuts were quietly evaporating — context that likely shaped the sharp individual pessimism visible in the data. Since then, the picture has only grown more complicated: April's Consumer Price Index came in hotter than expected at 3.7% year-over-year, wholesale prices jumped 6% annually, and the odds of a Fed rate hike before year-end leapt from near zero to roughly 45% in a matter of weeks. Equity markets had a split week — the S&P 500 briefly closed above 7,500 for the first time on Thursday, a record milestone, then fell sharply on Friday as investors weighed the inflation data and the arrival of new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh, who officially succeeded Jay Powell on May 15.
methodology: These indices come from the Yale/Shiller U.S. Stock Market Confidence Surveys, which have tracked investor sentiment since 1989. Each month, roughly 100 wealthy individual investors and 100 institutional investment managers answer the same standardized questions. The consistency of those questions over 35+ years is what makes the data meaningful — you can actually compare investor psychology across very different market environments. The four indices each measure something distinct: • 1-Year Confidence: The share who expect prices to be higher in 12 months. • Crash Confidence: The share who believe a crash in the next 6 months is very unlikely (under 10% probability). A low reading means most people think a crash is plausible. • Buy-on-Dips Confidence: The share who believe that after a 3% single-day market drop, prices would fully recover within two days. • Valuation Confidence: The share who do not believe the market is overvalued. Surveys were conducted semi-annually before 2001 and have been monthly since July 2001. Respondents rotate over time, but consistent characteristics — high income, investment experience — are maintained across the panel.
